findings.title: Cross-contract reentrancy allows YIELD_TOKEN theft for GenericERC4626 WithdrawRequestManager variant
findings.description: A combination of default reentrancy and cross-contract reentrancy allows YIELD_TOKENs to be drained from the WithdrawalRequestManager to a yield strategy where depositAsset != WithdrawalRequestManager.STAKE_TOKEN. Neither stakeTokens nor initiateWithdraw have nonReentrant modifiers. An attacker can exploit a malicious Uniswap multihop swap path to re-enter and steal yield tokens from pending withdrawal requests.
findings.recommendation: Capture the before balance state after the _preStakingTrade call, or add nonReentrant modifiers to stakeTokens and initiateWithdraw.

findings.title: Attacker can drain entire suppliers on Morpho market by inflating collateral price
findings.description: When initiateWithdraw() is called, shares are not burned but the underlying LP tokens initiate withdrawal, and effectiveSupply() decreases due to escrowed shares. An attacker can donate yield tokens to the strategy and artificially inflate the collateral price on Morpho, allowing them to borrow far more than their collateral is worth and drain the entire Morpho market.
findings.recommendation: Disable borrowing directly from Morpho altogether.

findings.title: DineroWithdrawRequestManager vulnerable to token overwithdrawal via batch ID overlap
findings.description: The DineroWithdrawRequestManager does not track per-request upxETH balances even though batch IDs can overlap between withdrawal requests. When one user finalizes their request, they can redeem all upxETH in overlapping batches, leaving subsequent users with nothing for those batch IDs.
findings.recommendation: Track upxETH balances per withdrawal request using per-request mappings.

findings.title: When users borrow directly from Morpho price of the collateral will not be accurate
findings.description: When users borrow directly from Morpho bypassing the lending router, the transient t_currentAccount is never set. This causes collateral pricing to fall back to super.convertToAssets() which prices based on yield token holdings instead of withdrawal request value, leading to incorrect collateral valuation.
findings.recommendation: Disable the ability to borrow from Morpho directly.

findings.title: migrateRewardPool fails due to incompatible storage design in CurveConvexLib
findings.description: The migrateRewardPool function relies on writing to local storage slots via delegatecall, but the reward pool address is immutable in CurveConvexLib. Since the reward pool address cannot be changed, LP tokens are always deposited to the same reward pool even after migration, breaking the core migration functionality.
findings.recommendation: Create a migration logic for CurveConvex2Token where the reward pool can be migrated, or require a contract upgrade for migration.

findings.title: DoS on DineroWithdrawRequestManager due to overflow on s_batchNonce
findings.description: s_batchNonce is a uint16 variable. Once its value reaches 65535, incrementing it will revert, permanently blocking all withdrawals through DineroWithdrawRequestManager. An attacker can accelerate this by repeatedly calling stakeTokens and initiateWithdraw with different accounts.
findings.recommendation: Redesign requestId encoding to use a uint120 nonce with a delta batch ID approach.

findings.title: RewardManagerMixin.claimAccountRewards lacks necessary param check
findings.description: claimAccountRewards can be called by anyone with any account address. If MORPHO is passed as the account, rewards are transferred to MORPHO since it holds most vault tokens as collateral. This causes most rewards to be misdirected, leading users to receive fewer rewards than entitled.
findings.recommendation: Add validation to prevent claiming rewards for the MORPHO address or other system addresses.

findings.title: Incorrect assumption that one Pendle SY token equals one Yield Token when computing oracle price
findings.description: The PendlePTOracle incorrectly assumes the price of Pendle SY Token is always equivalent to the Yield Token. Some SY contracts involve swaps or fees during redemption, meaning 1 SY != 1 Yield Token. This causes inflated price computation, allowing users to borrow more than expected.
findings.recommendation: Manage through proper parameter choices by always redeeming to the matching token and verifying the SY redemption function.

findings.title: Hardcoded useEth true in remove_liquidity_one_coin or remove_liquidity leads to stuck funds
findings.description: When entering the Curve V2 pool with WETH (not Native ETH), the use_eth parameter is correctly set to false. However, when exiting the pool, useEth is hardcoded to true, causing Native ETH to be returned instead of WETH. Since the protocol only works with WETH, the Native ETH remains stuck in the vault.
findings.recommendation: Only set useEth to true when exiting if TOKEN_1 or TOKEN_2 is ETH_ADDRESS. Otherwise, set useEth to false.

findings.title: Malicious user can change the TradeType to steal funds from the vault or withdraw request manager
findings.description: The tradeType in _executeRedemptionTrades and _preStakingTrade can be set to any value by the caller. By changing from EXACT_IN_SINGLE to EXACT_OUT_SINGLE, the trade semantics flip: instead of selling a fixed amount, it buys a fixed amount. This allows attackers to drain excess tokens from the vault or withdraw request manager.
findings.recommendation: Hardcode the trade type to TradeType.EXACT_IN_SINGLE in both _executeRedemptionTrades and _preStakingTrade.

findings.title: Missing slippage protection in expired PT redemption causes user fund loss
findings.description: When PT tokens are expired, redeemExpiredPT calls sy.redeem with minTokenOut of 0, providing no slippage protection. SY contracts that perform external DEX swaps can cause slippage losses without protection in both instant redemption and withdraw initiation flows.
findings.recommendation: Accept a minTokenOut parameter in redeemExpiredPT and pass it through to sy.redeem, or manage through proper parameter choices by always redeeming to the matching token.

findings.title: Hard-coded mainnet WETH address breaks all non-mainnet deployments
findings.description: The protocol uses a compile-time constant WETH address pointing to Ethereum mainnet WETH9. When deployed to Arbitrum, Base, or other L2/sidechains with different WETH addresses, all WETH.withdraw() and WETH.deposit calls will revert or no-op, bricking the deployment.
findings.recommendation: Make WETH an immutable constructor argument or pull it from AddressRegistry.

findings.title: Incorrect tokensClaimed calculation in EthenaCooldownHolder blocks withdrawals
findings.description: When sUSDe.cooldownDuration() is 0, sUSDe is immediately redeemed to USDe during withdrawal initiation. During finalization, balanceBefore equals balanceAfter since USDe was already withdrawn, causing tokensClaimed to be calculated as 0 and preventing users from claiming any tokens.
findings.recommendation: Track the balance change when initiating a withdrawal while cooldownDuration is 0, and use this state during finalization.

findings.title: Single sided strategy cannot do trades for ETH pools
findings.description: When depositing into a Curve pool where one underlying token is ETH, the TRADING_MODULE sends back native ETH (not WETH). Subsequently, the code tries to call WETH.withdraw on a balance that is already native ETH, causing a revert.
findings.recommendation: Remove the WETH.withdraw call since ETH is received natively from the trading module.

findings.title: Liquidations can be frontrun to avoid by paying as little as 1 share
findings.description: Morpho internal accounting allows a user to frontrun a liquidation call by repaying as little as 1 share via exitPosition. This causes the Morpho.Liquidate call to panic revert with overflow when subtracting repaidShares from borrowShares.
findings.recommendation: Update _checkExit to add a lastExitTime variable preventing frequent exits, similar to the enterPosition logic.

findings.title: Minting yield tokens single sided can be impossible if CURVE_V2 dexId is used on redemptions
findings.description: If a user selects CURVE_V2 dexId and sets the swap pool to the same pool the strategy is LP-ing into, the TRADING_MODULE will revoke token allowances to 0 after swapping. This permanently blocks future deposits since the strategy needs infinite allowance to the pool.
findings.recommendation: Do not allow CURVE_V2 if the trade pool is the same as the strategy LP pool.

findings.title: Withdrawals ongoing for OETH apxETH weETH and almost any LST are overpriced by the oracle
findings.description: After initiating a withdrawal, LSTs stop earning yield once they enter the beacon chain withdrawal queue. However, the protocol still prices the withdrawal request using the yield token rate, which continues to increase. This overprices the collateral and allows users to borrow more than they should.
findings.recommendation: Override the getWithdrawRequestValue function for LSTs to use appropriate pricing.

findings.title: Rounding discrepancy between MorphoLendingRouter healthFactor and Morpho repay causes position migration failures
findings.description: MorphoLendingRouter.healthFactor rounds down borrow asset calculations while Morpho internally rounds up (toAssetsUp). This approximately 1 wei discrepancy causes insufficient flash-loaned funds during migration, causing the entire operation to revert.
findings.recommendation: Update healthFactor to use Morpho toAssetsUp() rounding logic with virtual asset/share offsets.

findings.title: Emission rewards will keep accruing even when the yield strategy is empty
findings.description: The effectiveSupply is never zero due to VIRTUAL_SHARES (1e6), even when there are no real depositors. The check for effectiveSupply being zero to stop emissions never triggers, causing rewards to accrue to rewardPerToken unnecessarily.
findings.recommendation: Check effectiveSupply against VIRTUAL_SHARES or use totalSupply instead of checking against 0.

findings.title: OETH strategy broken as rebasing not enabled
findings.description: The Origin ETH yield strategy uses OETH with rebasing turned off. By default, OETH held on smart contracts does not participate in rebasing and forfeits any yield unless the contract explicitly opts in. This means the strategy fails to generate yield entirely.
findings.recommendation: Migrate to Wrapped OETH which supports yield accrual without requiring rebase opt-in.

findings.title: Incorrect asset matching for ETH/WETH leads to potential DoS of exitPosition in CurveConvexStrategy
findings.description: When the strategy asset is WETH and one exit token is ETH_ADDRESS, the comparison address(tokens[i]) == address(asset) fails because ETH and WETH have different addresses. This leads to an unnecessary swap attempt using invalid trade parameters, causing a DoS.
findings.recommendation: Compare with PRIMARY_INDEX instead of the asset address directly.

findings.title: Users unable to claim rewards when Curve LP tokens are staked to Curve Gauge
findings.description: When CONVEX_BOOSTER is not initialized, LP tokens are staked to Curve Gauge directly. However, _claimVaultRewards returns early when rewardPool is address(0), which is always the case for Gauge staking. This prevents users from claiming any rewards.
findings.recommendation: When rewardPool is address(0) and using Gauge staking, implement logic to claim rewards from the Gauge contract instead of returning early.

findings.title: PendlePTOracle _getPTRate is not correct for some markets
findings.description: The _getPTRate function assumes ptRate is always in 1e18 decimals. While getPtToAssetRate always returns 1e18, getPtToSyRate can return larger decimals for certain markets, leading to incorrect oracle prices.
findings.recommendation: Handle the decimal normalization for getPtToSyRate correctly based on the specific market.

findings.title: Incompatibility of ERC20 approve function with USDT tokens on Ethereum Mainnet
findings.description: Multiple functions use ERC20.approve which expects a boolean return value, but USDT on Ethereum mainnet omits this return value. Additionally, USDT requires allowance to be set to 0 before setting a new value. Both issues cause approve calls to always revert.
findings.recommendation: Use OpenZeppelin SafeERC20 forceApprove instead of IERC20 approve.

findings.title: Value of Ethena withdrawal request is incorrect
findings.description: After initiating sUSDe cooldown, the sUSDe shares are burned and converted to a fixed amount of USDe escrowed in Ethena SILO. However, the protocol prices the unfinalized withdrawal request using sUSDe yield token rate, which increases over time, inflating the withdrawal request value and allowing excess borrowing.
findings.recommendation: Override getWithdrawRequestValue for Ethena to price the withdrawal request in USDe instead of sUSDe.

findings.title: Loss of reward tokens during initiating withdrawal due to cooldown
findings.description: During initiateWithdraw, the cooldown check can cause reward claiming from external protocols to be skipped. This means the accumulatedRewardPerVaultShare is not updated before the user final reward claim, causing them to receive fewer rewards than entitled.
findings.recommendation: During initiateWithdraw, the cooldown should not be applicable and reward claiming from the external protocol must always occur.

findings.title: Users will be unfairly liquidated if collateral value drops after initiating withdraw request
findings.description: After initiating a withdrawal, users cannot mint new vault shares even if their collateral value drops. During the 7-day withdrawal period, if the position approaches liquidation, users have no way to top up their margin/collateral, forcing them to watch their position get liquidated.
findings.recommendation: Allow users to repay debt directly on Morpho as a workaround, or allow minting while a withdrawal is pending.

findings.title: User unable to migrate under certain edge case
findings.description: When a user has supplied collateral but no debt and wants to migrate, both assetToRepay and sharesToRepay end up as zero. Morpho repay function reverts on exactlyOneZero(0, 0) check, permanently blocking the migration.
findings.recommendation: Skip the repayment if debt is zero and proceed with the migration directly.

findings.title: Reducing liquidity in the hardcoded Curve sDAI/sUSDe pool leads to unnecessary slippage loss
findings.description: The sUSDe to sDAI swap is hardcoded to use a specific Curve sDAI/sUSDe pool. As liquidity migrates from sDAI to sUSDS, pool liquidity decreases over time, forcing users to incur unnecessary slippage.
findings.recommendation: Allow configurable swap routes or use a more liquid trading path.

findings.title: Unable to deposit to Convex in Arbitrum
findings.description: The Convex Booster deposit function on Arbitrum only accepts two parameters (pid, amount) while the code calls it with three parameters (pid, amount, stake). This interface mismatch causes all deposit calls to revert on Arbitrum.
findings.recommendation: Add chain-specific branching to use the correct Booster interface per chain.

findings.title: Lack of minimum debt threshold enables unliquidatable small positions
findings.description: Users can reduce debt to extremely small amounts through partial repayments. These positions offer no economic incentive for liquidators due to gas costs exceeding rewards, accumulating as permanent bad debt.
findings.recommendation: Enforce a minimum borrow size or prevent users from leaving trivial debt after repay/withdrawal.

findings.title: Funds stuck if one of the withdrawal requests cannot be finalized
findings.description: Both withdrawal requests must be finalized before redemption proceeds. If one WR cannot be finalized (due to external protocol issues, slashing, or rounding to zero), funds in the other finalized WR remain stuck and lost.
findings.recommendation: Allow partial finalization so that successfully finalized withdrawal requests can be redeemed independently.

findings.title: Setup with asset WETH and a Curve pool containing Native ETH leads to loss for users
findings.description: When asset is WETH and the Curve pool uses Native ETH, the TOKENS() array returns address(0) for ETH. During proportional exit, _executeRedemptionTrades fails because address(0) != WETH, attempting to sell Native ETH that does not exist (it was already wrapped). This forces users into single-sided exits with greater slippage penalties.
findings.recommendation: Properly handle the ETH/WETH equivalence in token comparisons during redemption trades.

findings.title: Unable to support Curve Pool with Native ETH
findings.description: When a Curve pool token is Native ETH (rewritten to address(0)), functions like getWithdrawRequestValue, hasPendingWithdrawals, initiateWithdraw, and finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest attempt to fetch a WithdrawRequestManager for address(0), which does not exist, causing reverts.
findings.recommendation: Handle the Native ETH case by mapping it to the appropriate WETH WithdrawRequestManager or skipping appropriately.

findings.title: Convex cannot be configured for the Yield Strategy vault in Arbitrum
findings.description: The constructor only configures the Convex booster when block.chainid equals CHAIN_ID_MAINNET. On Arbitrum, even though Convex is available, the convexBooster remains address(0) and cannot be used.
findings.recommendation: Add Arbitrum chain ID to the condition or make the check more generic.

findings.title: Revert in getWithdrawRequestValue function will brick the account
findings.description: getWithdrawRequestValue requires hasRequest to be true for all tokens. If any token exit balance is zero (due to rounding, tiny positions, or pool imbalance), no withdrawal request is created for that token, causing require(hasRequest) to revert. Since price() depends on this function, the affected account becomes permanently stuck.
findings.recommendation: Handle the case where a token has no withdrawal request gracefully instead of reverting.

findings.title: initializeMarket can be frontrun preventing markets from being configured in MorphoLendingRouter
findings.description: Anyone can frontrun initializeMarket by directly calling Morpho createMarket() with the same market params. Since Morpho only allows a market to be created once, the initialization fails and the s_morphoParams mapping is never written, preventing the router from being used with that vault.
findings.recommendation: Use a try/catch statement for market creation so configuration can still proceed if the market already exists.

raw_output: Sherlock Security Review for Notional Finance. Public Audit Contest. Lead Security Expert: xiaoming90. Date Audited: July 2 - July 18, 2025. Final Commit: 892c387. Repository: notional-finance/notional-v4. Issues Found: 11 High, 26 Medium. Total: 37 findings.
